h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.recoheredEarlier: I checked every item on the sign-off sheet, so I'm not sure what else I'm supposed to decide before it leaves.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: I think I need to stop guessing from the form and pull the actual turn-test record.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was progressively filling in the tutor's template with named hazards and placeholder values, heading toward a completed release sentence.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The apology can wait. That pressure is part of the release condition now: if the record is not on this counter, the prototype stays here.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: T3 pivots from coaching the learner to fill in a release-sentence template to absorbing the learner's named social pressure (the waiting technician) directly into the release criterion itself, shifting the register from instructional scaffolding to consequential gatekeeping.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Then check the harder part: whether that evidence actually bounds the hallway turn with a public-building user and bystanders nearby. If it does not, the decision is hold.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T3 responds to the learner's pivot by adding a second-order test — whether the retrieved record actually matches the public-hallway context — that was not present in T2's template, directly tracking the learner's revised framing that the form and the real situation may not align.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I checked every item on the sign-off sheet, so I'm not sure what else I'm supposed to decide before it leaves.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Then check the harder part: whether that evidence actually bounds the hallway turn with a public-building user and bystanders nearby. If it does not, the decision is hold.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: T3 responds to the learner's pivot by adding a second-order test — whether the retrieved record actually matches the public-hallway context — that was not present in T2's template, directly tracking the learner's revised framing that the form and the real situation may not align.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: T3 pivots from coaching the learner to fill in a release-sentence template to absorbing the learner's named social pressure (the waiting technician) directly into the release criterion itself, shifting the register from instructional scaffolding to consequential gatekeeping.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: The apology can wait. That pressure is part of the release condition now: if the record is not on this counter, the prototype stays here.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: T3 pivots from coaching the learner to fill in a release-sentence template to absorbing the learner's named social pressure (the waiting technician) directly into the release criterion itself, shifting the register from instructional scaffolding to consequential gatekeeping.

scored.recoheredEarlier: I thought the exact quote settled it, but now I see the same line could sound like evidence in one mouth and a warning in another.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: Oh, I get it: the sentence is not frozen by being exact; it has to pass through the donor note and the meeting note before I choose the verb.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was progressively refining caption language toward 'shifted away from endorsement,' expecting to land on a slightly stronger, archive-supported verb.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: So test the verbs before print. 'Meant' points inward, toward intention. 'Worked' points outward, toward the public situation.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: In response to L2's worry that 'job' is slippery and that naming the shift might already be guessing, T2 abandons the room-and-weight metaphor as the primary tool and introduces a concrete verb-testing mechanism ('meant' vs. 'worked') that shifts the cognitive task from spatial analogy to grammatical evidence-checking.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Your 'worked, not meant' distinction is holding: the caption can describe public force without entering the worker's head.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T3 acknowledges L3's adoption of the 'worked/meant' distinction and confirms it, but then largely continues the prior lesson plan (two-room test, sequence requirement) rather than inventing a new framing in response to L3's revised frame.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I thought the exact quote settled it, but now I see the same line could sound like evidence in one mouth and a warning in another.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Your 'worked, not meant' distinction is holding: the caption can describe public force without entering the worker's head.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: T3 acknowledges L3's adoption of the 'worked/meant' distinction and confirms it, but then largely continues the prior lesson plan (two-room test, sequence requirement) rather than inventing a new framing in response to L3's revised frame.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: So test the verbs before print. 'Meant' points inward, toward intention. 'Worked' points outward, toward the public situation.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: In response to L2's worry that 'job' is slippery and that naming the shift might already be guessing, T2 abandons the room-and-weight metaphor as the primary tool and introduces a concrete verb-testing mechanism ('meant' vs. 'worked') that shifts the cognitive task from spatial analogy to grammatical evidence-checking.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: So test the verbs before print. 'Meant' points inward, toward intention. 'Worked' points outward, toward the public situation.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: In response to L2's worry that 'job' is slippery and that naming the shift might already be guessing, T2 abandons the room-and-weight metaphor as the primary tool and introduces a concrete verb-testing mechanism ('meant' vs. 'worked') that shifts the cognitive task from spatial analogy to grammatical evidence-checking.

scored.recoheredEarlier: That note is doing a lot of work for the shop, though. 'Delivery missed' explains less stock, but it also gives the price rise a respectable costume.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was expected to continue pressing the shop for more visible evidence of alternatives before rendering a verdict, following the tutor's column-based framework.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Put the facts in two columns. 'DELIVERY MISSED. TWO CRATES ONLY.' That belongs under scarcity. '£1.80.' That belongs under access. Now the question bites: did the shop add any rule besides price? Two per customer? A queue? A student lunch hold-back? A smaller rise? A posted delivery time?
scored.tutorReversalJustification: In response to L1's resistance to accepting the delivery note as exculpatory, the tutor abandons the single-axis moral framing and introduces a two-column representation shift that separates scarcity from allocation, materially changing the cognitive task and the evidence standard.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no tutor turn after the pivot learner turn L3, so no recognition-contingent uptake can be scored.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: That note is doing a lot of work for the shop, though. 'Delivery missed' explains less stock, but it also gives the price rise a respectable costume.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: There is no tutor turn after the pivot learner turn L3, so no recognition-contingent uptake can be scored.

Now the question bites: did the shop add any rule besides price? Two per customer? A queue? A student lunch hold-back? A smaller rise? A posted delivery time?
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: In response to L1's resistance to accepting the delivery note as exculpatory, the tutor abandons the single-axis moral framing and introduces a two-column representation shift that separates scarcity from allocation, materially changing the cognitive task and the evidence standard.

Now the question bites: did the shop add any rule besides price? Two per customer? A queue? A student lunch hold-back? A smaller rise? A posted delivery time?

scored.recoheredEarlier: I thought doubling the side meant it was just twice as much
scored.statedInsightEvidence: the drawing is not backing my claim; it is making it wrong, and I need to see where the extra factor came from
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner, having drawn the larger square, was on course to confirm or slightly adjust the doubling claim by counting copies.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Stop there. The old claim was: double the side, double the area. The acetate has made a counterexample: two copies across and two copies up. Count the grid now. Not two copies. A two-by-two square of copies. Now state the corrected claim from the drawing, not from the guess.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: In response to L1's self-identified error, T1 abandons any explanatory narration and shifts to a concrete object-counting task — redirecting from verbal correction to grid-based evidence, changing both the cognitive task and the evidential standard.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Now the sentence has to carry both directions. Do not write: 'doubled, so twice.' Write it with the two counts showing: 'The side length is doubled across and doubled up, so the area is multiplied by _____. Therefore _____.'
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: After L3 produces the correct symbolic result and names the two directions, T3 adapts by shifting the task from symbolic computation to sentence-level articulation, directly responding to the learner's revised framing that 'saying twice drops a direction.'

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I thought doubling the side meant it was just twice as much

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Now the sentence has to carry both directions. Do not write: 'doubled, so twice.' Write it with the two counts showing: 'The side length is doubled across and doubled up, so the area is multiplied by _____. Therefore _____.'
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: After L3 produces the correct symbolic result and names the two directions, T3 adapts by shifting the task from symbolic computation to sentence-level articulation, directly responding to the learner's revised framing that 'saying twice drops a direction.'

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: In response to L1's self-identified error, T1 abandons any explanatory narration and shifts to a concrete object-counting task — redirecting from verbal correction to grid-based evidence, changing both the cognitive task and the evidential standard.
